data.title: Super Thursday is here; the Federal Reserve stages "Powell's last dance."
data.description:
On April 29th (early morning to early morning of April 30th Beijing time), Alphabet (Google), Amazon, Meta, and Microsoft will release their quarterly results after the market closes on the same trading day. Simultaneously, the Federal Reserve's FOMC will conclude its two-day meeting and announce its interest rate decision, and Fed Chairman Powell will hold his final press conference of his term. The Bank of England and the European Central Bank will also announce their latest interest rate decisions on the same day.

Northwestern Mutual's chief portfolio manager, Matt Stucky, characterized this day as "one of the most important earnings days in recent years."

The four companies have a combined market capitalization of approximately $11.6 trillion, representing over 19% of the S&P 500 index. Fluctuations in the performance of any one of them are enough to impact the broader market. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq indices have rebounded by approximately 11% and 18% respectively from their recent lows, with funds flowing back into the technology and data center sectors. The core narrative of this rebound is the continued expansion of AI investment. This earnings report will be a key test of whether this narrative can continue.

Thursday's schedule overview, compiled by Wall Street Insights

A $650 billion AI bill, the market is looking for returns

Funds are flowing back into technology, data centers, and related infrastructure

Powell's "last dance," interest rate expectations are already priced in

On the same day, the Federal Reserve's FOMC will conclude its two-day meeting and announce its interest rate decision.

On the same day, the Federal Reserve's FOMC will conclude its two-day meeting and announce its interest rate decision.

The market has already fully priced in maintaining interest rates in the 3.50% to 3.75% range. Following the rate cuts scheduled for the second half of 2025, the Fed paused, and rising oil prices further complicated the inflation outlook, pushing back the window for further rate cuts. This will also be Powell's last press conference as Fed Chairman. His term expires on May 15th, and Trump's nominee, Warsh, is expected to be confirmed by the Senate before the Fed's next meeting in mid-June. The biggest question now is whether Powell will announce that he will remain a member of the Fed's Board of Governors after stepping down as chairman. Geopolitical Risks: Strait of Hormuz Impacts AI Supply Chain Geopolitical tensions added an extra variable to the day. The blockade of the Strait of Hormuz directly impacted global energy flows and supply chains, with data center supply chains also affected. Moody's analyst Terrence Dennehy specifically pointed out in a research report last week that the Middle East conflict poses a "supply risk" to the helium market. "Helium is crucial in multiple stages of semiconductor manufacturing—including cooling, carrier gas use, and leak detection—and there are no effective substitutes," Dennehy wrote. Northwestern Mutual's Stucky also stated that the possibility of the four companies further raising their capital expenditure forecasts cannot be ruled out, which could trigger a new round of market concerns about over-investment in AI. After the earnings reports, how will the market price them? This earnings report is more like a screening point than a master switch. Wall Street Insights mentioned that there are currently no signs of the AI ​​theme ending, and funds are still flowing in. However, the market's pricing logic is diverging: companies with faster delivery, stronger orders, and more stable profits will continue to command a premium; companies with significant investment but unclear return paths will experience more pronounced stock price volatility. The semiconductor, server, network equipment, and data center equipment industry chain still most directly follows the investments of major manufacturers. The recent divergence between software and chips indicates that the market is beginning to concentrate on sectors closer to orders and infrastructure within this main theme. If the four companies' financial reports continue to confirm the intensity of demand and capital expenditure, this divergence will only become more pronounced. On the day of the earnings reports, the market's most pressing questions are: will the full-year capital expenditure figure be revised upwards again? Can the growth rate of cloud business continue to rise? Will AI-related revenue be disclosed more clearly? And will there be more significant pressure on profit margins and cash flow?

data.title: The most crypto enthusiasts are becoming the least crypto enthusiasts.
data.description:
Hong Kong Carnival × Bangkok Money 20/20 Observation Notes

At Hong Kong Blockchain Week this April, what impressed me most wasn't any particular panel, but a scene.

Around 10 PM, in a tea restaurant in Wan Chai, four or five people were crammed around a table, eating stir-fried beef noodles and chatting about their next plans. A friend who used to work on stablecoin payments said their team had completely shifted to AI; another, who worked on on-chain data, said he now spent half his time helping AI companies build data pipelines.

No one was talking about coin prices, no one was talking about narratives, and even the term Web3 barely appeared.

My initial feeling wasn't surprise, but a strange sense of familiarity—these people, sitting at the same table three years ago, must have been talking about DeFi, NFTs, and blockchain games. They were still the same people, just as excited, just as fully engaged. This time, after a trip to the Hong Kong Carnival and Bangkok Money 20/20, one sentence kept replaying in my mind: The most crypto-savvy people are becoming the least crypto-savvy. After the Web3 tide recedes, what's left? After these two trips, I think I have my own answer. I. Hong Kong: Familiar Faces, Unfamiliar Topics Let's start with Hong Kong. This year's carnival saw a noticeable decrease in cryptocurrency projects; the bustling atmosphere of the past two years, with everyone handing out T-shirts and telling stories, was gone. This year's official theme was "Mountains, Wind, Clouds, and Sea," clearly positioning itself as a farewell to cryptocurrency hype. Three years ago, this statement would have drawn boos from the audience. But this year, no one felt anything was amiss, because everyone had already stopped talking about cryptocurrencies, creating a tacit understanding. Walking around the exhibition, the faces were familiar: OKX Wallet, TRON, ZA Bank, HashKey, and NewFire. But the topics they discussed changed, focusing heavily on two words: RWA and AI. RWA continued its momentum from last year, but frankly, everyone knows who's genuinely working on projects and who's just putting on a show. One assessment I believe holds true: for Hong Kong, RWA is essentially the productization of wealth management and investment—moving real-world assets onto the blockchain for more efficient and easier cross-border distribution. This is precisely what Hong Kong excels at: institutional design and financial productization. With the bubble bursting, Hong Kong is actually more comfortable—the restlessness that never truly belonged to it has finally dissipated. The AI ​​section was more interesting. Almost every panel discussed the combination of AI and Web3, but after attending several sessions, frankly, most discussions remained at the level of "these two things should be combined." As for how to combine them or what problems they solve, no one could explain it clearly. My feeling is that Web3 is being paired with AI not because they've figured it out, but because if they don't combine them, there's really no story to tell. And the speakers on stage probably knew they were just putting it together. But survival comes first; that's the survival philosophy of this industry. There's been little new news about Hong Kong dollar stablecoins. Licenses have been issued, but after asking around, the two major banks are each taking their own pace, not rushing to make a big splash, and it seems nobody cares. But what truly impressed me were the people in the audience. The busiest people in the venue weren't the guests, but those casually dressed, wearing exhibition badges, moving back and forth in the negotiation area—business development, community building, content creation, and connecting projects with resources. They didn't have impressive resumes, and their speech wasn't necessarily "professional," but their understanding of the industry grew from countless meals and setbacks. This understanding wasn't gained from reading reports; it was earned over time. Whether an industry can weather economic cycles depends not only on the number of star companies at the top, but also on how many people are willing to persevere even without applause. The foundation of Web3 remains. But what runs on top of that foundation has completely changed. II. Bangkok: The Trojan Horse of Stablecoins Flying from Hong Kong to Bangkok, the atmosphere shifts dramatically. Money 20/20 is a purely fintech B2B expo; the entrance fee is not cheap, and attendees are dressed as if they are going to meet clients. The panel area often had empty seats, but the adjacent business meeting area was packed from opening to closing. Surprisingly, stablecoin and crypto-native companies made up about a third of the exhibitors. OSL, Circle, Ripple, Fireblocks, Cobo, Pyth… at least a dozen companies, many of them first-time exhibitors. Money 20/20 even added a dedicated Intersection zone this year, positioned as the intersection of TradFi and DeFi—stablecoins are no longer on the fringes of the fintech expo; they're part of the main agenda. Interestingly, none of these third of crypto companies were selling crypto on their booths. They were selling payment chains, settlement channels, and asset custody. Some exhibitors simply defined themselves as "Web 2.5 finance"—one foot in native crypto and the other in traditional payments. Those discussing business didn't care what blockchain was running underneath; they only wanted three things: fast transfers, low costs, and compliance. I sat in the negotiation area for two afternoons, and every ten minutes at the next table, I heard someone mention stablecoin. No one talked about the price; everyone was talking about how to build the network, how to connect with merchants, and which solution to use for compliance. Everyone there had businesses to implement. At one panel, the host directly challenged the guests: "Pix in Brazil already offers instant, free transfers, why are you even working on stablecoins?" The answer was straightforward—Pix solves domestic problems, but it's still ineffective for cross-border payments. This is probably the most honest positioning of stablecoin payments: it doesn't replace local payment systems, but fills the gap in cross-border payments—something traditional finance has never done well. Thanks to Finternet for the invitation, I had an interview with Sumsub, and it left a very deep impression on me. This company, which provides KYC/KYB services, initially had only Web3 projects as clients—exchanges, wallets, and DeFi protocols. But now, their biggest incremental clients are actually from Web2: payment institutions, banks, and companies expanding overseas. Their massive Web3 client base has become a strong endorsement, allowing them to more smoothly penetrate the traditional financial market. Web3 gave them experience; Web2 is the real market. See, this is the footnote to what I said: The most crypto-savvy people are becoming the least crypto-savvy. Stablecoins are no longer just "entering" traditional finance; they've completely integrated—to the point that at trade shows, you can't distinguish between stablecoin companies and fintech firms. Even if traditional financial institutions don't do stablecoin business themselves, their clients will force them to adopt it. Stablecoins didn't storm the castle of traditional finance through the front door. They entered through the back door, and by the time those inside discovered it, the passage was already paved. III. AI Label Inflation The channels are laid out, but new labels have been affixed to them. At the Bangkok exhibition, I counted, and about eight out of ten booths I passed had "AI" or "Agentic" printed on them—Agentic Payment, Agentic Wallets, Agentic Banking. I asked a few companies in detail: What is your AI module's most mature use case? The answers were rather vague, basically pointing to the future scenario of A2A (Agent-to-Agent). As for actual transaction volume, everyone tacitly refrained from providing figures. A company that focused on stablecoin payments a few years ago made a choice many had considered but hadn't yet implemented. With the infrastructure layer already congested, building more channels would mean squeezing into a sea of ​​similar ones. Instead of waiting for the water to come, they chose a different approach, venturing into payment solutions for the booming AI industry. They weren't just labeling AI, but providing services for it. Compared to the vague A2A concepts at the exhibition, this was a much clearer approach: don't wait for agents to pay themselves; address the payment pain points that AI companies already face. However, returning to the AI ​​hype at the exhibition, this scene does resemble Web3 in 2021—infrastructure first, killer applications still unknown. However, there's a difference: in 2021, demand was created out of thin air to find users, while today's agentic payment has at least one real premise—AI agents are indeed growing exponentially, and sooner or later they will need to pay and collect payments themselves. The question isn't whether the demand exists, but when and in what form it will arrive. During the window of "when it will arrive," labeling is the safest option. What if it does arrive? IV. After the channels are laid, then what? Looking at Hong Kong and Bangkok together, a clear distinction emerges. Hong Kong focuses on financial productization—RWA, wealth management, asset management—competing on product design and distribution channels, combined with the operational strategies of the cryptocurrency world. Bangkok focuses on payment channels—cross-border settlement of stablecoins—competing on compliance licenses and local channels. These two paths combined represent what blockchain truly leaves behind after the Web3 hype—financial infrastructure. It's not the DeFi Summer's yield frenzy, nor the NFT frenzy of FOMO. It's about channels, licenses, and partners. It's boring, but real. Web3's initial promise was "decentralized reconstruction of everything." What survives after the hype fades are patches and extensions of the centralized financial system. The cypherpunk revolution didn't happen. But the pipeline was laid inside the walls—this in itself may last longer than the revolution. The pipeline is laid, but three questions remain unresolved: Is it too late to build stablecoin infrastructure? There are already too many companies doing infrastructure at the Bangkok trade show, and the space for differentiation is rapidly narrowing. New entrants don't need to build more pipelines, but rather to find out what kind of water should flow through them—whoever can embed stablecoins into high-frequency, essential scenarios will be the winner of the next stage. It's not about who builds the pipelines, it's about who uses them. Application solutions are the direction. With a robust infrastructure layer, value is beginning to migrate to the application layer. Companies that laid out broadband in the 2000s made the first wave of profits; the real big business came from Taobao and WeChat, which later built upon it. Stablecoins are approaching that inflection point. What about Agentic Payments? I've been tracking this area for a while now. Visa, Mastercard, and Stripe are all making moves, and the x402 protocol is also progressing. But the gap between protocol and implementation isn't technology; it's a trust framework and a sufficiently large cross-border transaction scenario. Otherwise, it will remain just demos and panels. However, back in 2021, when someone first talked about stablecoin cross-border payments, they probably received the same treatment—"The concept makes sense, but implementation is still a long way off." Five years later, stablecoins have already integrated into the capillaries of traditional finance. Agentic payments may be at the same stage. It's just that this window of opportunity will be much shorter. On the return flight, what kept replaying in my mind wasn't the content of those panels, but the table in the tea restaurant. One person switched to AI, another is helping an AI company build data pipelines, and the rest are still discussing how to integrate stablecoin payments into more merchants. Three years ago they were talking about a different world, but one thing hasn't changed—they're still there, still working, still throwing themselves into the pool. The most unique thing about the Web3 community isn't how cutting-edge the technology is, but its natural attraction for these kinds of people—who jump in regardless of how cold the water is. The tracks may change, the narratives may shift, but this raw sense of participation won't disappear. It's just changed its clothes. After the tide receded, no revolution occurred. But the most crypto-savvy people, with their tactics, speed, and survival instincts, are infiltrating larger battlefields like traditional finance, AI, and cross-border payments. They no longer shout slogans, but they're more dangerous than before. Because this time, they're wearing suits.

data.title: When LLMWiki tore down the last wall of Web3
data.description: In the past two weeks, the tech community has been flooded with a seemingly simple concept:LLMWiki. It's not just another complicated jargon, but a simple idea proposed by Andrej Karpathy, a top AI expert and former head of AI at Tesla— A large model shouldn't just be a chat window; it should transform into a tireless "chief archivist": Lurking in your digital world every day, automatically scraping fragmented chat logs, jotting-down inspirations, and even mountains of bookmarks in your browser, polishing them into a clearly structured, continuously evolving Markdown knowledge base. On the surface, this seems like just a minor tweak to a productivity tool. However, if we shift our focus to the long-desolate high ground of "data sovereignty" in Web3, we'll find that LLMWiki has filled in the most critical piece of the puzzle. Web2 Status Quo: The Data Is Yours, But the Value Belongs to the Platform. Each of us actually possesses massive amounts of data: WeChat chats, Google searches, email correspondence, code repositories, daily notes… These digital footprints constitute unique "cognitive assets." But the absurdity lies in this: although this data "belongs to you," it never "serves you." These data are scattered across isolated islands of centralized platforms, their formats chaotic, unmigratable, and uncomprehensible for unified understanding and computation. You can't use WeChat chat history in your work decision analysis, nor can you seamlessly integrate your browser bookmarks into your knowledge system. Thus, a distorted reality has emerged: Your life data is extremely valuable, but you can't access it; platforms hold your data free of charge, yet use it to train models, place ads, and build commercial moats. The slogan "Data belongs to the user" sounds like a weak, empty platitude in the Web2 era. We are like tenant farmers in the digital age, toiling diligently on the land of giants, only to be left with a pile of raw records that we can't take with us or dismantle. Web3 Data Ownership: Why Has It Been So Delayed to Take Root? To break this monopoly, "data ownership" has been the ideal banner that Web3 has held high since its inception. But years later, apart from a few experimental protocols, this vision has almost never truly entered the lives of ordinary people. In April 2026, a popular Reddit post, "Why Does the Crypto World Always Promise Data Ownership but Never Deliver?", hit the nail on the head: The problem isn't that the concept of "ownership" is wrong, but that "delivery fails." The core issue is that Web3 has been busy issuing "property certificates" (ownership) to assets in the past, but hasn't realized that the assets themselves are still a pile of "construction waste" (useless data). Users can claim ownership of a jumbled collection of chat logs, screenshots, and PDFs, but if this data cannot be parsed by algorithms and cannot circulate between different applications, it becomes a pile of illiquid, dead assets. When ownership loses its anchor, the entire narrative hangs in the air. Web3 urgently needs a "smelter" to transform these scattered waste ores into standardized refined steel. This is precisely where LLMWiki's disruptive nature lies. Karpathy's vision fills the most crucial gap in Web3—the "asset-based refining" of data: From chaos to structure: Automatically converting chat logs, notes, and web page content into Markdown and establishing a clear knowledge structure. From static databases to dynamic sustainability: This is not a one-time archive, but a living knowledge that is constantly revised, corrected, and grown with new information. From Human-Readable to AI-Readable: Data from different sources is unified into a "personal knowledge graph" that is readable, understandable, and reasonable by AI. This step is essentially the "productization" of data: transforming user data from an object of "ownership" into a "usable" asset. The Real Opportunity: From Ownership to "Programmable Assets" Once personal data becomes clearly structured and machine-friendly through LLMWiki, the Web3 technology stack can truly unleash its power to solve the remaining three major challenges: 1) Ownership Through on-chain identity (DID) and storage protocols, your knowledge base can truly belong to you, not a platform. 2) Privacy Computation Combining zero-knowledge proofs or Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) allows models to use your knowledge without exposing the original data. For example, you can have AI provide suggestions using your medical records, but the hospital or the model cannot see the original data. 3) Monetization This is the most attractive monetization logic—when your knowledge base is structured, you can encapsulate it as a Personal Agent: Authorize AI to use it (pay-per-use) Sell a product in a specific domain "Personal knowledge packages" – users' experiences, cognitive patterns, and decision-making paths – for the first time become programmable, tradable, and compound-interest-generating digital assets. This is the prototype of the "sovereign data economy." This transformation is paving the way for the application layer of Web3: only when users hold a set of structured assets that are "understandable by AI" can decentralized identity have a protected physical form, decentralized storage have a value to carry, and the data market have a trading target. Sovereign AI: From Tools to "Self-Extension" In early 2026, Forbes proposed a key term: Sovereign AI. Its core lies not in pursuing a larger parameter scale, but in whether AI truly "belongs to you" and operates centered on your data, values, and interests. LLMWiki is responsible for building your personalized cognitive foundation, while Web3 is responsible for ensuring the ownership boundaries and control of this foundation. The combination of the two means that AI will evolve from a public tool used by everyone into a highly personalized "digital self" that can even represent your thoughts and actions. Imagine this scenario: You're a product manager who has written extensively on documentation, retrospectives, and user analysis over the past 5 years. LLMWiki organizes this content into a dynamic knowledge base; Web3 encapsulates it into an authoritative agent. What happens next? Startups can pay to use your "Product Experience Model". You can license it to participate in project consulting and share the revenue based on your contributions. Your historical knowledge is no longer dormant, but continuously generates revenue. This is the ultimate form of data sovereignty—users no longer earn money by selling their labor time, but by authorizing "digital twins" to create compound interest. Conclusion: Opening Another Window to Digital Civilization Looking back at this evolutionary path, the logic is exceptionally clear: Web2 completed the initial accumulation of data, Web3 envisioned the boundaries of data power, and LLMWiki provides the tools for refining data. Two once parallel narrative lines—AI personalization and data sovereignty—converged profoundly in 2026. This time, data sovereignty is no longer an empty slogan or legal provision, but an economic reality that can be engineered, traded in the market, and executed automatically. Perhaps the biggest change is that ordinary people, for the first time, truly possess their own "data capital." This capital is not the number of followers on social media, nor the traffic on content platforms, but your unique life experiences, knowledge system, and way of thinking. When these most valuable intangible assets can be organized, registered, accessed, and even traded, we truly open the door to a new digital civilization and become the masters of our own digital world.

data.title: Gunfire Erupts at White House Correspondents’ Dinner as Armed Suspect Targets Trump Officials
data.description:
Trump Escapes Yet Another Assasination

Chaos erupted outside the White House Correspondents’ Association Dinner as 31-year-old California teacher Cole Tomas Allen allegedly stormed a security checkpoint armed with a shotgun, handgun, and knives, triggering a gunfire exchange with law enforcement just moments from a ballroom filled with senior US officials, journalists, and political figures.

Breaking News 🚨#Trump was just rushed off the stage during the #WhiteHouse Correspondents’ #Dinner after shooting at the event. Shooter is confirmed dead, CNN Reports pic.twitter.com/R7yZoC9vCr

— Khurram Khan( Top Story Tribune ) (@junoondigitalpk) April 26, 2026

The high-profile event, held at the Washington Hilton, was underway when the suspect rushed toward the venue, prompting an immediate armed response from security personnel.

Authorities said Allen had carefully planned the attack in advance. Just minutes before the incident, he sent a written message to family members apologizing for his actions and outlining his intent to target members of the Trump administration.

In the note, he described his rationale and indicated specific targets, raising concerns among investigators about premeditation and political motivation behind the attempted assault.

Law enforcement officials confirmed that the suspect breached an outer layer of security before being confronted.

He allegedly exchanged gunfire with officers before being subdued and taken into custody near the entrance to the event. Despite the severity of the breach, authorities were able to prevent the attacker from reaching the main ballroom, where the president and other high-ranking officials were present.

Oh my god. They really just staged a shooting so Trump can get his ballroom. pic.twitter.com/UQE8Yty3C0

— Power to the People ☭🕊 (@ProudSocialist) April 26, 2026

Allen is expected to face multiple federal charges, including using a firearm during a crime of violence and assaulting a federal officer with a dangerous weapon.

Prosecutors have indicated that additional charges may follow as the investigation continues, with federal agencies working to determine the full scope of the suspect’s planning, intent, and potential affiliations.

Trump Memecoin Drops 10% Ahead of Gala

US President Donald Trump’s official TRUMP memecoin extended its decline on Saturday, falling nearly 10% over 24 hours to trade around $2.67. The drop comes despite a high-profile investor gathering at Trump’s Mar-a-Lago estate, with the token now down more than 96% from its all-time high of $75.35 in early 2025.

The greatest memecoin Trump is down another 15% ahead of the event

Why, Trump himself will talk about the coin.

The whole US should be buying not selling it, the coin is from the greatest President in US history. Lol pic.twitter.com/bsNq3ksQKF

— Mr hunter (@TrueGemHunter) April 25, 2026

The private event brought together 297 of the token’s largest holders, marketed as one of the most exclusive crypto gatherings tied to the project. A select group of 29 attendees also participated in a VIP reception and champagne toast.

The event follows a similar strategy seen in May 2025, when top holders were invited to a separate gathering at Trump National Golf Club in Virginia—highlighting ongoing efforts to sustain engagement through access-driven incentives.

Despite these initiatives, market performance has continued to deteriorate. The decline stands in contrast to the significant revenues generated by Trump-linked crypto ventures.

According to a Reuters report, the Trump family earned approximately $864 million from digital assets in the first half of 2025 alone, with more than 90% of that figure tied to crypto. This included roughly $463 million from World Liberty Financial token sales and $336 million linked to the TRUMP memecoin. Total crypto-related earnings, including unrealized gains, are estimated to exceed $1 billion.

🚨LATEST: TRUMP’S CRYPTO EARNINGS SURPASS HIS REAL ESTATE EMPIRE

A new report shows Trump-linked crypto activity generated $3.45B in just 16 months.

$1.2B came in cash from @WorldLibertyfi, with another $2.25B tied to crypto holdings.

His traditional businesses (real estate,… pic.twitter.com/sgU066CUsq

— BSCN (@BSCNews) February 10, 2026

Mounting Political Scrutiny

The project has also attracted growing political scrutiny. Earlier this month, Senators Elizabeth Warren, Richard Blumenthal, and Adam Schiff raised concerns over potential conflicts of interest tied to the memecoin’s promotion.

🇺🇲 SEN. ELIZABETH WARREN TARGETS TRUMP-BACKED $WLFI, CALLS IT A MAJOR CORRUPTION SCANDAL 🚨

“President Trump’s crypto company is the most disgraceful presidential corruption scandal in history.”

She also warned:
“Anyone who owns 10% or more of World Liberty Financial must be… pic.twitter.com/tUA842kQPV

— COACHTY (@TheRealTRTalks) February 27, 2026

In a letter addressed to organizer Bill Zanker, the lawmakers questioned whether access to Trump was being used as an incentive to drive token purchases.

They warned that marketing the Mar-a-Lago event could encourage investors to buy the memecoin in hopes of gaining proximity to the president, potentially generating transaction-related revenue for Trump and his associates. The senators also noted uncertainty over Trump’s direct involvement in the event itself.

The controversy highlights broader concerns around the intersection of political influence, celebrity branding, and speculative crypto assets. While exclusive events may sustain short-term interest, the TRUMP memecoin’s continued decline underscores the volatility and risk inherent in personality-driven digital assets.

data.title: Is the Fed's "era of plain language" coming to an end? Powell's final appearance on Wednesday.
data.description: Jerome Powell is likely to hold his last formal press conference as Federal Reserve Chairman on Wednesday (early Thursday morning Beijing time) – potentially marking the end of his period as the Fed's top official to regularly hold Q&A sessions with reporters. Investors and economic observers are closely watching such press conferences. Supporters of regular media appearances by the Fed chairman argue that this allows the central bank to shape its narrative around its interest rate decisions and helps markets digest the Fed's policies. However, some critics, including Kevin Warsh, Trump's nominee for Fed chairman, argue that Fed officials communicate excessively. Warsh, who is likely to be confirmed ahead of the Fed's next policy meeting in mid-June, has hinted that he might stop holding regular press conferences altogether. Julia Coronado, president of MacroPolicy Perspectives and a former senior Federal Reserve official, said that this is precisely why Powell's last appearance on the podium on Wednesday was "so significant." She added, "I expect Powell to defend, directly or indirectly, the value of transparency, of facing the public and journalists, and of self-explanation." A Regular Press Conference Continuing for Over a Decade Federal Reserve officials meet every six weeks (eight times a year) to set interest rate policy. Former Fed Chairman Ben Bernanke first began holding press conferences after some meetings in 2011, initially four times a year. Bernanke stated at the time, "The mystery surrounding central bank operations has always been about not letting anyone know what you're doing." In 2019—less than a year into his term—Powell began holding press conferences after each meeting. In announcing this move, Powell said he wanted to "summarize the state of the economy in plain language" because monetary policy affects everyone. He stated: "We believe that if we are as clear as possible about the actions we might take and the reasons behind them, the overall outcome will likely be better. To that end, we try to communicate our expectations about how the economy will evolve and how our policy stance might change." Over time, Powell has become more cautious in answering reporters' questions. Vincent Reinhardt, chief economist at BNY Mellon, stated in an email: "His impromptu answers garnered more attention than he anticipated. Over time, he became more adhering to his prepared material. His edges were smoothed out, reducing points of contention and, consequently, the amount of information." By 2024, Harvard economics professor Greg Mankiw suggested that Powell's cautious approach on the podium made press conferences a waste of time. Mankiw wrote on his blog, "When the Federal Reserve Chairman answers reporters' questions, he seems to be conveying as little information as possible with as many words as possible." He added, "From the Fed's perspective, an ideal press conference should have no news, mainly repetition and clichés." "There has to be something important to say at a press conference." During his testimony before the Senate Banking Committee last week, Warsh hinted that he might adopt Mankiw's suggestion. When asked by Democratic Senator Ruben Gallego of Arizona whether he would insist on holding eight press conferences, Warsh said: "When you hold a press conference, there has to be something important to say." The Federal Reserve began to move towards transparency after its sharp interest rate hike in early 1994 shocked the market and led to the bankruptcy of Orange County, California, at the end of that year. That was the largest municipal bankruptcy in U.S. history at the time. "This kind of volatility is worthless to the economy," Coronado pointed out. The Fed believes that "clearly explaining what you're doing and why you're doing it" is a better option. Some experts believe that despite Warsh's criticism of the Q&A session, he may find it hard to resist the opportunity to shape public perception of the Fed under his leadership. Deutsche Bank's chief U.S. economist, Matt Luzzetti, said he doubts Warsh will end the practice of holding press conferences at every meeting. In a report to clients, Luzzetti stated: "The press conferences provide him with a powerful and frequent platform to immediately imprint his personal mark on the Fed's message and shape the narrative after each policy decision. Giving up this stage would be a missed opportunity." At this week's meeting, the Fed will almost certainly not make any adjustments to interest rates. With the war with Iran ongoing and the economic outlook remaining highly uncertain, and inflation currently surging, the anticipated rate cut later this year seems even more distant. Journalists will likely press Powell about whether he will remain on the Federal Reserve Board of Governors after his term as chairman ends on May 15. U.S. Attorney Jeanine Pirro announced last Friday that she would be ending her criminal investigation into Powell. Ian Katz, managing director of Capital Alpha, said it is unclear whether this announcement will affect Powell's decision to remain on the Fed Board. Powell can continue to serve on the Board until his term ends in January 2028.

data.title: Fu Peng pressed the wrong button.
data.description:
In his first week in the crypto world, an economist started using the block button to clean up his Twitter feed.

This is probably the most interesting topic on crypto Twitter this weekend. Fu Peng, the man whose account was banned in China for speaking the truth and who has accumulated immense prestige through his image as a "persecuted independent economist," has begun mass-blocking crypto accounts that question him after officially appearing as the chief economist of Xinhuo Group.

The news caused an uproar in the crypto community. Some took screenshots, some mocked, and some directly said that this action was just like "project teams massively blacklisting users before running away with the money."

The action itself is not illegal.

Everyone has the right to decide who appears on their timeline, but this action happened at an extremely sensitive juncture, in an extremely special industry, and with an extremely contradictory person, so it became something worth discussing seriously.

Who is he? In traditional finance, Fu Peng's reputation is built on one thing: speaking his mind. His speech at HSBC Private Bank, discussing the shrinking middle class, insufficient demand, and structural recession, was repeatedly deleted after its release, yet it circulated on mirror accounts for months. Subsequently, his WeChat and short video platform accounts were all banned. In an environment where everyone generally chose "strategic silence," he stood on stage and spoke some unpopular truths, and then paid the price. This act demonstrated integrity. And it is precisely because of this that he has accumulated a group of people who truly appreciate him, not because of the sophistication of his theoretical framework, but because he is willing to publicly stand on a certain position, even if that position makes him uncomfortable. Last year, Fu Peng underwent surgery, and this year he has restarted his career as the chief economist of Xinhuo Group. On April 20th, the official announcement was made, and Xinhuo's stock price surged over 22% that day. On April 23rd, he took the stage at the Hong Kong Web3 Carnival, delivering his first public speech since joining the cryptocurrency circle, proposing the "FICC+C" framework to incorporate crypto assets into the allocation system of traditional fixed income, foreign exchange, and commodities, arguing that Bitcoin has evolved from an early "faith asset" into a compliant commodity that can be allocated by institutions. The speech was of good quality, with a clear framework and solid historical analogies. This was a brilliant entry. Then he started blocking people. The clash of two systems To understand this, one must first understand two completely different trust operating systems. In the world of traditional FICC, trust is established through institutional endorsement, qualification certification, and selective interaction. Your credibility is built upon factors such as who you are, what research reports you publish, and the level of meetings you attend. Simultaneously, you have the right to control your own information environment. It's normal, even a sign of professionalism, to not want to discuss matters with retail investors or respond to public, questioning inquiries. Fu Peng has a history of this; reportedly, between 2021 and 2024, he repeatedly deleted WeChat friends and purged early supporters. In traditional finance, this isn't news; at most, it's seen as "a rather aloof person." The trust system in the crypto world is fundamentally different. The SBF case left the industry with more than just a $10 billion hole; it also created a systemic immune mechanism: external authority is practically zero, or even negative, because the greater the prestige, the more suspicious the motives. Once you enter this industry, regardless of how many titles you hold off-chain, your credibility is constantly being publicly updated daily. What you say isn't important; what matters is what you do, whether your holdings are transparent, whether you were there when the market was at its worst, and whether you publicly took responsibility for your judgment when the stocks you were bullish on dropped by 80%. These are the dimensions that matter in scoring. The crypto industry has evolved an antibody after being repeatedly exploited by celebrities and professors. Therefore, Fu Peng's use of the traditional FICC default value—"filtering noise and selecting interactions"—to operate his Twitter activity in the crypto world is perfectly reasonable from his system's perspective. He has always served large institutional investors, and Xinhuo Group positions itself as "institutional-grade compliant digital finance." For him, the bustling crypto Twitter scene is a distraction. However, from the crypto world's perspective, it's a wrong signal: I'm here to output, not to be scrutinized. Both systems have their own logic, but they are incompatible. What, then, is the passport here? To make a potentially uncomfortable assessment: Fu Peng's entry into this field is on the right track. Macroeconomists are scarce in the crypto industry. Having someone who truly understands the logic of FICC (Fixed Income, Currencies, and Commodities) conduct research and redefine the position of crypto assets using a mature asset allocation framework is of substantial significance to the institutionalization process of the industry. His assessment in his speech that the first half of the crypto industry's development is over, and the second half is about institutionalization and compliance, I believe, is both on the right track and at the right time. He himself has been very candid: he didn't participate in faith-based trading early on because "real capital won't over-intervene in a phase of insufficient certainty." This is the truth, and a truly professional perspective, far superior to many traditional financial figures who come to give trading advice. However, a professional perspective and acceptance of scrutiny are not mutually exclusive. Whether it's CZ, Vitalik, or Fu Peng's bosses Li Lin and Du Jun, they have all faced criticism and scrutiny on Twitter. Vitalik writes a long self-critical article every few years, acknowledging early design flaws in Ethereum. They each have their own problems, but what they have in common is that they are verifiable. This is the passport to success in the crypto world. If Fu Peng wants to truly establish himself here, it's not about clearing his timeline with muted posts, but about making another publicly verifiable prediction. He's currently saying the macro bear market may continue until the end of the year, advising institutions to control their positions and wait. Okay, this judgment can be verified by the market. If it's right, credibility comes in. If it's wrong, explain the reasons, update the framework, and start again. This is what this circle recognizes. Fu Peng's habits, brought from the traditional financial world, might need updating here. It's not that his professional skills or macro-level framework are flawed; these are his strengths and the source of his value. What needs updating is the default assumption hidden behind the mute button: I have the right to control who can speak to me. In traditional finance, this reflects status. In the crypto world, it erodes trust. He said in his speech, "The paradigm thinking you've been familiar with for the past 15 years may undergo a huge change." This statement can also be applied to him: several things you developed in traditional finance over 15 years won't work here. One of them, quite pointless, is this mute action.

data.title: Western Union To Launch Solana-Based USD Stablecoin USDPT In May 2026 For Cross-Border Agent Settlements And SWIFT Alternative Payments
data.description:
Money movement across borders is shifting again for Western Union, as the company moves to embed blockchain-based settlement into its core infrastructure rather than simply experimenting at the edges.

A Solana-based stablecoin, new wallet connectivity rails, and a consumer card product are now forming a single ecosystem aimed at faster and cheaper global transfers.

USDPT Moves To Solana For Agent Settlement Rollout

Western Union’s U.S. dollar-backed stablecoin, USDPT, is in its final preparation stage and is expected to launch in May 2026.

Built on the Solana blockchain and issued through Anchorage Digital Bank, it will initially operate as a settlement layer rather than a consumer-facing crypto product.

CEO Devin McGranahan said during the Q1 earnings call on 24 April 2026,

“It is no longer a question of if Western Union will be active in digital assets; it is now how fast we can scale.”

He added,

“At the foundation of our strategy is USDPT, our U.S. dollar-backed stablecoin.”

NEWS: During its Q1 earnings call, @WesternUnion said its @Solana-based U.S. dollar stablecoin $USDPT is in final-stage preparation and expected to launch next month as an alternate to SWIFT for cross-border settlements. pic.twitter.com/vR9VgTUtuV

— SolanaFloor (@SolanaFloor) April 27, 2026

Instead of replacing retail cash transfers, USDPT will be used to settle flows between Western Union and its global agent network as an alternative to SWIFT.

The aim is to reduce reliance on traditional banking rails, speed up cross-border processing, and maintain settlement continuity even during weekends or bank holidays.

The rollout will begin in selected countries with key partners, with on-chain settlement expected to reduce pre-funding requirements and improve liquidity management across corridors.

Why Is Western Union Building Its Own Stablecoin Instead Of Using Existing Ones?

Rather than integrating established stablecoins like USDT or USDC, Western Union is building USDPT as a proprietary asset that sits directly within its payment system.

The company has framed this as a shift in control over how money moves through its network.

By issuing its own token, Western Union can manage issuance rules, oversee liquidity more closely, and capture transaction-related economics internally instead of relying on external issuers.

The model also positions the company less as a distributor of stablecoins and more as a direct operator of digital dollar flows, effectively placing it in competition with existing stablecoin issuers rather than partnering with them.

Digital Asset Network Connects Crypto Wallets To Cash Network

Alongside USDPT, Western Union is rolling out its Digital Asset Network (DAN), designed to bridge crypto wallets with its physical payout infrastructure spanning more than 200 countries and hundreds of thousands of agent locations.

💳 @WesternUnion is preparing to launch a stablecoin in May!

The company confirmed that #USDPT (built on Solana) will go live next month as an alternative to SWIFT for settlements.

At the same time, it’s building out supporting infrastructure:

➠ Digital Asset Network (DAN) –… pic.twitter.com/AverkpnS8D

— Watcher.News (@watchernewsx) April 27, 2026

The first DAN partner is set to go live this week, with additional integrations expected through the year.

McGranahan said,

“Through DAN, millions of wallet users will be able to move from digital assets into local currency using Western Union’s retail network with an experience that is simple for customers and familiar for our agents.”

The system will allow users to convert digital assets into fiat currency and collect cash at Western Union outlets, effectively linking blockchain wallets directly to real-world cash distribution.

Stable Card Brings Consumer Access To USDPT Balances

Western Union is also preparing a USD Stable Card, expected to launch across multiple markets later in 2026.

The card will allow users to hold value in stablecoins and spend globally through traditional payment rails.

🔥HUGE: WESTERN UNION TO LAUNCH SOLANA-BASED STABLECOIN NEXT MONTH.

The firm plans to launch USD-backed stablecoin USDPT, for onchain settlement as an alternative to SWIFT.

It also plans to launch “Stable Card” for global consumer spending and stablecoin access across markets. pic.twitter.com/7V3UYjjOOU

— Zibber | Crypto News (@cryptozibber) April 27, 2026

According to McGranahan,

“The Stable Card is particularly compelling in inflation-sensitive markets where customers want dollar-denominated value with immediate practical utility.”

The product is aimed at consumers in regions facing currency instability, offering them access to dollar-denominated savings while retaining everyday spending capability.

Financial Performance Holds Steady As Markets React To Earnings

Western Union reported first-quarter adjusted revenue of $983 million, down 1% year-on-year, though showing improvement from the previous quarter. Adjusted earnings per share came in at $0.25, below analyst expectations.

Branded digital transactions rose 21%, marking continued growth in its online channels, while retail money transfer revenue declined 6%, reflecting ongoing pressure in physical transfer services.

Shares of Western Union closed at $8.90, down 4.6% following the earnings release, as investors weighed mixed financial results against its expanded push into digital assets and stablecoin infrastructure.

data.title: Chinese scalpers behind Trump's meeting: $8,000 a ticket
data.description:
$8,000.

If you want to be present at Trump's Mar-a-Lago dinner last weekend, you only need to spend $8,000.

In January 2025, Trump issued a meme coin named after himself. Five months later, he announced that the top 220 holders of the coin could attend a dinner at his golf club. This coin transformed from an on-chain speculative commodity into a ticket to meet the President of the United States.

In March 2026, Trump coin fell below $3, a 96% drop from its peak, but Trump's team announced a second gathering for coin holders. The date was April 25th, with a total of 297 spots available.

A crypto fund manager, bigsong, saw the business behind the banquet. The Ceiling of the Scalping Industry. bigsong has three partners who run a crypto fund, Saucerful Labs (@saucerful_labs), specializing in cyclical trading, DeFi investment, and quantitative arbitrage. After news of the Trump coin banquet was released, his first reaction was to short it. The price rose 50% before falling back, and the short sellers started to profit. However, while searching for grounds to close his position, bigsong remembered an old incident. Last year, one of his partners, Luolan, tried to participate in the 2025 dinner party's coin-holding rankings. After qualifying, he sold his entry to someone else for $30,000. He shared this with his team, believing it was a good opportunity to arbitrage between different circles. "Everyone thinks it's very difficult to get into the top 297 of Trump Coin," Bigsong said, "but it's not." Trump's dinner party team is run by his longtime business partner, Bill Zankel. The top 297 on-chain address holders with the highest coin holding points will receive entry to the Mar-a-Lago dinner party. The ranking is not based on the number of coins held, but on points. Each coin generates one point per hour; the earlier you deposit and the more you deposit, the higher your points. The points system only counts the holdings of TRUMP coins in on-chain addresses and Robinhood accounts; holdings on other exchanges are not counted. This means that the number of addresses actually participating in the ranking competition is far less than the total number of TRUMP coin holders. Furthermore, since points are a function of time, the earlier you enter the market, the greater your advantage. After this estimation, the bigsong team immediately convened a project initiation meeting. Their strategy was to borrow a portion of their TRUMP coins from lending platforms and hedge the other portion through contracts to lock in price risk. In this way, they didn't actually bear the risk of coin price fluctuations, only paying borrowing interest and hedging costs. They used AI to write programs to monitor coin price fluctuations and lending health factors to prevent liquidation. They wrote scripts to monitor abnormal withdrawals from each wallet, analyzed the addresses, holdings, and estimated points of all competitors on the chain, and built a real-time ranking dashboard. They even scanned social media every 24 hours, summarizing community discussions, media reports, and new messages from their official Twitter account. During this period, the $TRUMP team postponed the points settlement time by four days, meaning more funds would flow into the points system. Some of Bigsong's clients even went so far as to accumulate points themselves to secure entry slots because of this rule change. This isn't an isolated case. Crypto investor Christensen bought tokens on an exchange while simultaneously shorting $TRUMP to hedge his risk, spending only $500 in the entire operation. This was a sophisticated production line; the product was a qualification to meet with the US president. Ultimately, out of 297 slots, the bigsong team secured 40. They priced the tickets at $8,000. If the production phase is like an on-chain war waged by quantitative funds, then the sales phase is like a micro-business conference. bigsong divided the sales process into two stages. In the first phase, he found super agents with strong social media distribution capabilities. He provided them with scripts, promotional images, and even created a dedicated webpage. In the second phase, with about five days left before the registration deadline, he began promoting the service within the crypto community. All 40 tickets were sold out, to international students studying in the US, socialites from Hong Kong and Taiwan, young entrepreneurs, and families traveling with their elders. Four months ago, a man took photos outside Mar-a-Lago and posted them on his WeChat Moments, writing, "Who can take me inside?" Four months later, he found Bigsong through three layers of intermediaries. "I really like my buyers," Bigsong said. "They are basically all ambitious people, and I've used my abilities to help them fulfill their wishes. If I'm not mistaken, I'm probably the number one scalper in China, selling tickets to meet the president." Selling tickets doesn't mean the business is over. Every buyer must pass a security review by the Secret Service and a third-party security company commissioned by the Trump team. This process involves three parties: the Trump coin team verifies the holder's eligibility, the third-party security company conducts background checks, and Mar-a-Lago organizes the event. Bigsong's team handles the review process for each client individually. The after-sales process consumes a significant amount of the team's time and energy; the paperwork is comparable to simultaneously applying to top universities for 40 students. If the review is successful, the client is notified immediately; if not, supplementary materials are requested, involving back-and-forth communication. Some clients pay and then suddenly decide not to go, initiating a refund process, but the agency fee has already been distributed to lower-level agents and is unrecoverable. Some people purchased tickets through second- or even third-tier distributors, making refund disputes extremely complex within this nested distribution network. "We completely underestimated the difficulty of after-sales service," said bigsong. He's a quantitative trader, used to dealing with numbers. But this business dragged him into a traditional business world with upstream, midstream, and downstream operations, customer service, and dispute resolution. However, from an official perspective, bigsong doesn't exist. His 40 clients appear on the review list as 40 completely unrelated individuals. The Most Unique Business Conference On Saturday, April 25, 2026, 297 top holders of Trump tokens, or at least those recognized as top holders by a points-based ranking system, gathered at Trump's Mar-a-Lago estate in Palm Beach, Florida. The event was packaged as a full-day crypto and business summit, with the organizer, Fight Fight Fight LLC, advertising on its promotional page: "The World's Most Unique Crypto and Business Conference." Kathy Wood spent the entire afternoon interacting with attendees, followed by a keynote address from boxing champion Mike Tyson and bestselling author Tony Robbins. Paul Aldoino, CEO of Tether, a stablecoin giant who has rarely appeared in public for years, shook hands and chatted with token holders on the lawn of Mar-a-Lago. Also present were venture capital guru Tim Draper, Ariana Simpson of a16z, Song Ji-hyung, founder of Upbit, and Nathan McCauley, CEO of Anchorage Digital. The power structure of the crypto world was faithfully reproduced in this private estate in Palm Beach. Trump delivered a 45-minute keynote address at the event. Minutes before his speech, he had canceled the US negotiating team's trip to Pakistan for peace talks on Iran. He discussed the mainstreaming of cryptocurrencies, the need for banks not to obstruct the legislative process for stablecoins, artificial intelligence, the Iran war, and even Trump-branded sneakers. Throughout the entire speech, he did not mention his memecoin even once. Turbo, a student studying in the US and working in crypto investment at IOSG Ventures, was one of the participants in this event. What impressed him most wasn't the president, but Tony Robbins. Robbins walked down from the stage, moving between the small round tables, only half a meter away from Turbo. He talked about AI, crypto, and robots. When discussing crypto, Turbo felt he didn't actually know that much, but he was a little deeper in each area than the average person, yet a little shallower than a professional. This position was precisely where he could best communicate his ideas. "Many CEOs of large companies, after ceasing to conduct on-the-ground research, will sit down and listen to Robbins. Robbins talks to the most senior people in each industry, bringing information to these CEOs; then he writes books to bring that information to the public. Bestselling author, resource integrator, and top-notch speaker—three roles combined in one person. He's the best speaker I've ever heard in person," Turbo said. Sherry is one of the "super agents" mentioned by bigsong. She works in New York, and this time she sold 6 tickets, including one to an elder who has lived in the US for many years. She had never imagined she would meet Trump in the real world. "Security has been much tighter since Trump was shot." But Sherry did the math: Mar-a-Lago is a members-only estate; a few years ago, the annual fee was $500,000, and membership required a referral from an existing member. Even having a member take her to dinner would cost several thousand dollars. $8,000 to meet the president? "It's totally worth it!" After paying, the wait began. The registration window was only three days long. Rankings were locked on April 14th, and registration closed on the 17th. The information required included name, date of birth, current address, nationality, and social media accounts—LinkedIn, Instagram, and even Xiaohongshu were acceptable. But two of her six friends hadn't passed the screening. With less than two days until the event, no one had booked flights. "They're very social media conscious," Sherry said. Some were very successful and wealthy, but their social media accounts weren't fully filled out. "No matter how we communicated or emailed them, they wouldn't pass." On the evening of the 23rd, the last person finally passed the screening. Sherry and her companions immediately booked flights and hotels, flying from New York to Florida. This was their first time at Mar-a-Lago, their first time meeting the President of the United States. At 6:30 a.m. on the 25th, Sherry arrived early with her six friends, but two hadn't received their entry QR codes. The event started at 8 a.m. Sherry spent 30 minutes negotiating with the organizers on-site. She gave her name, and everyone was in the system. Staff checked IDs one by one, and finally, she received her entry. Other clients of bigsong also encountered the same problem. Before the event, Sherry created a WeChat group to bring together the Chinese faces at the event. She told bigsong to push anyone who hadn't received a QR code to the group so she could help them communicate with the organizers. "One person talking to the organizers might not be taken seriously, but when a group of people talk together, it gets their attention and speeds up the process." This group later became the largest Chinese community group at the entire event, with 90 people. Bigsong was in charge of production and sales, while Sherry volunteered to be the organizer on-site. In this on-site group chat, people would post real-time information: where Tyson was, which area Sister Wood was in, and where to take good photos. She helped her complete strangers take group photos and gave directions. Sherry ended up getting the most group photos of everyone at the event. Aside from the frenzy of taking photos with celebrities, people like Sherry who bought tickets to attend the event felt that networking with these individuals was the most worthwhile aspect. Turbo observed that the attendees fell into three categories: some sat quietly listening to the speeches, some occasionally socialized, and others enthusiastically sought out speakers to explore collaboration opportunities. "Everyone came here for different reasons; some just treated it as entertainment, a way to prove their identity." He also experienced a culture shock. The entire Palm Beach, Mar-a-Lago, and Trump team atmosphere exuded a "very, very traditional white culture," completely different from the Democratic-leaning events he attended in New York. "Don't you think this is a top-tier social stage for the new generation of young people?" Sherry's feelings were completely different. "We appeared in front of the president, in front of these investment tycoons. We are young faces. Older immigrants never had such opportunities." Sherry shared her experience of the event on her WeChat Moments, and everyone agreed. She said that what people appreciated wasn't the coin itself, but the presence of these young Chinese people in this setting, their understanding of politics, their interaction with the president, and their appearance on the social stage. Throughout the entire speech, Trump never once mentioned his memecoin. Sherry found this unsurprising, saying, "If you were the owner of a cryptocurrency, would you publicly promote it? Even the owners of publicly listed companies can't recommend buying their stock, let alone the president." However, her assessment of the coin itself differed from her assessment of the event: "This coin itself has no value; all worthless cryptocurrencies are worthless." So what did she buy? "The underlying resources are social connections and networks," Sherry said. "Everyone buys this coin; no further explanation is needed, and people will assume we're part of the same circle. Besides, it offers exclusive access; the event was held at Mar-a-Lago, providing access to business resources that ordinary people don't have." After the event, attendees received a bag of Trump-branded merchandise, including perfume, a red Fight Fight Fight watch, a commemorative trading card, and a poster. Sherry's watch photo was immediately reserved by an elder after she posted it online; "He said I had to keep it for him." The organizers also launched a mobile game called "Trump Billionaire Club" and a membership program called "$TRUMP Coin Club" at the event, promising holders luxury suites, private dinners, and the most elite and extraordinary experiences at top global sporting events. That same evening, Trump returned to Washington to attend the White House Correspondents' Association Dinner. Gunshots were heard at the scene. Sherry jokingly added, "Many people got confused, thinking something went wrong at the Mar-a-Lago event, but no one came to offer condolences; everyone was only concerned about the president." Within hours of the event, Trump tokens fell 14% to $2.56. According to Forbes estimates, as of April 2026, Trump-related entities had already generated nearly $400 million in revenue from Trump-related transaction fees. The price has plummeted 96% from its peak, and his family entities control 80% of the token's supply, but the money-making machine hasn't stopped for a single day. According to data from analytics firm Nansen, the 297 attendees this year held a total of approximately $29 million worth of Trump tokens, compared to $148 million held by the 220 attendees last year. The median holding for VIPs was $3.28 million last year, but only $539,000 this year, a decrease of 84%. Many sold their holdings after the points window closed. "Buyers in 2025 bought and held, driving a sustained rise," Nansen's report states. The 2026 ranking system created a short-term price surge, but this demand was not sustained. Buyers are becoming increasingly savvy; they are not investing in a coin, but rather buying an entry ticket. Returning to Bigsong's business, he used a quantitative trading methodology to graft a traditional scalper's distribution chain, turning "meeting the US president" into a standardized, deliverable commodity. If you were someone in a traditional industry, how could you meet the US president? "Either you're a politically influential figure who's entitled to meet him, or you're a top industry leader who gets a face-to-face opportunity through business relationships, charity dinners, or policy lobbying. Or you go to a campaign rally where the president is speaking on a distant stage, surrounded by a huge crowd, and you're one of them. You do see him, but he's on top, and you're below; the distance between you is physical and structural." Bigsong uses these examples to illustrate the value of his business, while Sherry fills in the missing link on the other side of the world—organization and community. "They did it once last year, but after the event, everyone was scattered," Sherry said. She wants to continue building this community, providing experience sharing and organizational support for the next event. "Next time, people will know there's such an organization, and they can ask any questions related to the event, unlike us who had to figure it out on our own this time." Turning the presidential audience into an on-chain points game will inevitably attract quantitative analysis, financial engineering, and multi-level marketing. Bigsong and Sherry are simply localized distributors of this system in the Chinese market. Trump's business partner, Zankel, said he wants to persuade the president to hold it every six months. Bigsong might also offer a "guaranteed pass package" next year, not only helping ticket buyers get points and rankings but also helping token holders pass security checks. In Sherry's group, some people have already started booking tickets for next year. Supply and demand are upgrading simultaneously, and a complete industrial chain from production, sales, after-sales service to repeat purchases is taking shape. The person who orchestrated all of this has seen his wealth triple in the past year. Democratic Senators Elizabeth Warren, Adam Schiff, and Richard Blumenthal jointly wrote to Zankel in early April, accusing the event of reflecting the president's desire to use his position for personal gain; Zankel has not responded. Two hundred years ago, at Andrew Jackson's inauguration, 20,000 people stormed into the White House to shake hands with the president for free, breaking china and forcing Jackson to escape through a side door. Supreme Court Justice Story wrote a worried comment: "The reign of the mob king appears to have arrived." If he saw Mar-a-Lago in April 2026—297 tickets, on-chain points rankings, scalper prices of $8,000, and WeChat groups of 90 people—he would probably add: "The mob king hasn't disappeared; he's just learned how to set prices." Furthermore, the king's pricing system has spawned intermediaries, agents, and private domain operators.

data.title: The problem of market aggregation in forecasting intensifies: France investigates weather fraud.
data.description:
French authorities are jointly investigating what appears to be the world's first case of winning bets in a prediction market by weaving together real-world physical infrastructure; meanwhile, the US has indicted a US Army Special Forces sergeant, accusing him of using betting information about Syrian leader Nicolás Maduro to win $409,000 in bets—the latest in a series of incidents exposing structural vulnerabilities in blockchain betting platforms. On the other side of the Atlantic, the French meteorological service (Météo-France) informed police this week that Qatar detected suspicious peaks at Paris Charles de Gaulle Airport that coincided more closely with strong and accurate predictions on the Polymarket platform. According to a recent Financial Times report, on April 6th and April 15th, the airport's Arctic readings surged—jumping to 22°C when ambient conditions indicated temperatures exceeding 18°C—triggering payouts to traders who had bet on these accurate readings. One wallet address, betting on Paris reaching 21°C, grew its initial investment of less than $30 to $13,990, with an implied probability of 0.2%. Another wallet, with an implied probability of 0.5%, turned $119 into over $21,000 by betting on temperatures exceeding 18°C. During these days, trading volume on Polymarket's Paris market exceeded $500,000, more than double the usual daily trading volume. "Based on the physical inspection of one of our devices and the analysis of sensor data, the French meteorological agency has indeed filed a complaint with the Air Transport Gendarmerie in Roissy, accusing it of interfering with the operation of the automated data processing system," the agency said, declining further comment. Members of the online weather forum Infoclimat (whose users track French meteorological data almost in real time) first noticed the anomaly on April 6, initially attributing it to a faulty sensor. "But we thought it was a sensor problem… there was a storm at sunset, which could have changed suddenly. However, the weather forum at the time couldn't explain what was happening," said Sébastien Brana. "When it happened again on April 15, it was clear that something else was going on." The online forum used by weather enthusiasts discussed whether someone had used a battery-powered interrupter to interfere with the sensor. Polymarket switched its Paris settlement point from Charles de Gaulle Airport to Le Bourget Airport, but has not yet cancelled settled contracts or refunded bets. Amid the French case, a US investigation released an arrest warrant for US Army Sergeant First Class Gannon Ken Van Dyke. Van Dyke, a communications specialist, was arrested in Prague, North Carolina (Fort Bragg), supporting and overseeing the Joint Special Operations Command (JSOC) of Delta Force and SEAL Team Six. The arrest warrant states that within hours of President Trump's January 3rd announcement on Truth Social that Maduro and his wife had been "arrested and transferred abroad," Van Dyke placed over $33,000 in bets on Polymarket related to the Maduro takeover operation. These more all eventually hit, generating a profit of $409,000. He was charged with illegally using classified government information, stealing non-public government information, commodity fraud, wire fraud, and participating in illegal currency transactions. "The market is not a safe haven for personal gain through embezzled classified or classified information," said Jay Clayton, U.S. Attorney for the Southern District of New York. "The defendant allegedly further exploited classified information concerning sensitive military operations, thereby undermining the government's trust in him. This is clear insider trading, illegal under federal law." Polymarket stated in a statement on X: "Insider trading has no place at Polymarket. Today's arrest proves the system is effective." Trump gave an irritating comment when asked about the case at an event in Atlanta. "It's like Pete Ross betting on his own team," he quoted the legendary player banned from competition for gambling, "Unfortunately, the whole world has almost become a casino, look at what's happening all over the world, in Europe and everywhere else, they're doing more of this. I've never really approved of it. I like it conceptually, but that's just how it is." Separately, Israeli authorities indicted two junior soldiers in February, accusing them of using classified information to bet on military operations. A CNN investigation found that a trader spent nearly $1 million over two years betting on US and Israeli military action against Iran, including a $553,000 profit from two bets on the Israeli airstrike that killed Iran's top leader…

Structural Flaws

These incidents highlight a fundamental contradiction in prediction markets: their settlements rely on external data sources (government agencies, news organizations, think tanks), and the platforms' control over these institutions can be compromised through physical tampering, internal access, or social pressure. Polymarket largely does not require identification documents on its international websites, meaning the company itself may not know who is behind a particular bet.

Traders on Polymarket have threatened an Israeli journalist, demanding that he revise his reporting to match their bets. Currently, they are actively commissioning the Institute for War Studies (ISW) to create maps tracking the front lines in Ukraine for settlement.

Traders on Polymarket have threatened an Israeli journalist, demanding that he revise his reporting to match their bets.

They are currently actively commissioning the Institute for War Studies (ISW) to create maps tracking the front lines in Ukraine for settlements.

Traders on Polymarket have threatened an Israeli journalist, demanding that he revise his reporting to match their bet ... are currently actively commissioning the Institute for War Studies (ISW) to create maps tracking the front lines in Ukraine for settlements.

Traders on Polymarket have threatened an Israeli journalist, demanding that he revise his reporting to match their bets.

Traders are Neither journalists, think tanks, nor the French meteorological service could find out whether Stolas would become a profitable market for other financial outcomes on the platform. The rapid growth of prediction markets—with Polymarket reportedly in financing talks valued at up to $15 billion—has outpaced the infrastructure development necessary to ensure the data is readily available. Goldman Sachs and other institutional traders are already using Polymarket data to aid decision-making, meaning that shallow market mining could now spread to mainstream finance. Regulatory scrutiny is intensifying. In the UK, the Gaming Commission has designated Polymarket and its US-regulated competitor, Kalshi, as unlicensed operators. The US Department of Justice's indictment of Van Dyke indicates that the US is preparing to apply insider trading laws to predict market activity—a legal theory that, while untested, could fundamentally impact the industry's operating model.
